text: The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, carefully separating adhesions within the surgical field during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy procedure.

The surgical setup includes one port placed in the epigastric region, another port positioned along the midclavicular line just below the gallbladder fundus, and a fourth port located 7.5 centimeters lateral and below.

The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to hold the fundus of the gallbladder. Due to the patient's grade II fatty liver, there is reduced space between the rib cage and liver, but the fundus is successfully pushed towards the right shoulder.

The surgeon uses a left-hand grasper to apply anterior traction to the omental adhesions while pushing the gallbladder fundus towards the right shoulder. The harmonic device is employed to perform adhesiolysis, facilitating the separation of adhesions.
The surgeon performs additional adhesiolysis using a harmonic scalpel, positioning the silicon jaws towards the gallbladder to prevent vibration transfer and avoid gallbladder rupture.
The surgeon employs a stripping technique, holding the gallbladder and carefully stripping it down from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon holds the Hartmann's pouch of the gallbladder, revealing adhesion between the gallbladder and the duodenum.
The surgeon strips the adhesion bands that are keeping the duodenum adhered to the neck of the gallbladder, pulling down the adhesions to separate the structures.
The surgeon identifies Rovier's sulcus, a landmark on the liver surface near the gallbladder, which is visible in the surgical field.
Rovier's sulcus is visible, separating the right lobe of the liver from the caudate lobe at the level of the porta hepatis.
The surgeon ensures that the window formation and tissue separation occur above Rovier's sulcus, maintaining a clear boundary between the anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs aditiolysis to separate the posterior peritoneum, carefully detaching it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs peritoneal dissection to facilitate the proper dissection of the cystic pedicle, aiming to expose the colored triangle.

During the laparoscopic cholecystectomy, the surgeon performs peritoneal dissection at the level of the 'mouth of the elephant,' where the posterior peritoneum attaches to the body of the gallbladder.

The surgeon positions the silicon jaw of the harmonic scalpel towards the gallbladder and uses its tip to knock the peritoneum, facilitating the formation of a blade. As the hydrogen bonds open, the posterior peritoneum visibly separates from the liver at the level of the gallbladder body, ensuring the dissection remains above the Rouviere's sulcus.
The surgeon applies anteromedial traction to the Hartmann's pouch while dissecting the posterior peritoneum, facilitating the separation of the peritoneal tissue from the gallbladder.
The surgeon proceeds to dissect the anterior peritoneum during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy, focusing on separating it from the gallbladder.
The surgeon applies anterolateral traction to the gallbladder, facilitating the dissection of the peritoneum from the gallbladder body.

The surgeon adjusts the fundal traction while applying anterolateral traction. Using the tip of the harmonic device, the surgeon knocks the peritoneum, forming a blade that facilitates the automatic separation of the peritoneum, creating a clear dissection plane.
The surgeon uses the harmonic device to create a posterior window by turning its tip with the convex edge towards the liver, lifting it to separate the artery from the liver tissue.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to create and enlarge the anterior window in the peritoneum, facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon uses the harmonic device, noting its sharp nature, which is typically not used for creating or enlarging windows during the dissection process.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector, positioning its convex edge towards the liver to enlarge the previously created window in the tissue.
The surgeon uses the full jaw of the Maryland dissector, which measures approximately twenty-two millimeters, to manipulate tissue during the cholecystectomy procedure.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection to separate the cystic duct from the cystic artery, using a 2.2 cm space to apply three clips. The indentation between these structures becomes visible after stripping the peritoneum.

The surgeon uses the tip of the Maryland dissector to create an indentation between the cystic duct and cystic artery. The jaw of the instrument is initially opened slightly and then gradually widened to a full 22 millimeters, allowing space for the application of three clips. The cystic artery is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses the Maryland dissector to grasp and lift the cystic duct, creating tension that allows for better visualization of the duct. The full jaw of the Maryland is opened to facilitate the dissection.
The surface of the liver is visible, showing its smooth texture and coloration as the surgical field is exposed.
The surgeon identifies the common bile duct (CBD) in the surgical field, visible as a distinct anatomical structure.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery, which is visible after separating it from the cystic duct using the Maryland dissector.
The surgeon identifies the cystic duct, using the Maryland dissector to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon creates a posterior window in the surgical field, providing a clear view of the anatomical structures behind the gallbladder.
The surgeon exposes the anterior window during the cholecystectomy procedure, revealing the anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon points to the duct, highlighting its location in the surgical field.

The surgeon loads a titanium clip onto the clip applicator and positions it to clip the artery. The cystic pedicle is visible, appearing erect when relaxed, while the tented common bile duct (CBD) disappears from view.

The surgeon applies the first titanium clip to the artery using a clip applicator. The clip is held in place for a few seconds before the applicator jaws are disengaged and removed. Subsequently, the second and third clips are applied in a similar manner.

The surgeon uses Hem-o-lok clips for securing the artery, ensuring effective hemostasis. The clips are applied with precise spacing: three millimeters between the first and second clip, and six millimeters between the second and third clip, allowing for easy cutting between them.

The surgeon applies titanium clips to the artery, ensuring a three-millimeter distance between the first and second clips. Scissors are then used to cut the artery, with both limbs of the scissors clearly visible during the procedure.
The surgeon cuts the duct and creates a window for it. Mishra's knot is applied to the duct, providing secure closure without the risk of cat eye stone formation.

The surgeon feeds twenty centimeters of suture through the abnormal anatomy, using a Hartsman pouch grasper to hold the loop and prevent shearing effects. Outside the surgical field, the Misra's knot is tied with a sequence of one hitch, one wind, one lock, repeated twice more.

The surgeon applies a secure extracorporeal knot over the cystic duct, demonstrating its ability to hold structures up to 22 millimeters in diameter. The knot creates a visible dumbbell effect on the duct.
The surgeon applies an additional clip to secure the cystic duct or artery during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate the cystic duct, with a single clip applied above the duct to prevent bile leakage during the cutting process.
The surgeon separates the gallbladder from the liver bed, using surgical instruments to carefully detach the gallbladder from the surrounding liver tissue.
The surgeon applies anteromedial and anterolateral traction to the gallbladder, facilitating its separation from the liver bed.
The surgeon applies anteromedial traction, pushing the neck of the gallbladder anteriorly and medially towards the falciform ligament.
The surgeon applies anterolateral traction to the gallbladder, pulling it laterally to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon uses anteromedial and anterolateral traction to separate the gallbladder from the liver, completing the dissection between these structures.
The surgeon maintains a clear areolar plane while separating the gallbladder from the liver, ensuring no rupture of the gallbladder or bleeding from the liver occurs.
The surgeon uses a grasper in the left hand to periodically adjust traction on the gallbladder, ensuring a good areolar plane is maintained while slowly separating the gallbladder from the liver.
The surgeon continues to separate the gallbladder from the liver, utilizing a good plane of dissection that allows for separation without the use of energy devices.
The surgeon uses various instruments, including a hook, spatula, and scissors with monopolar cautery, to separate the gallbladder from the liver, demonstrating different techniques for dissection.
The surgeon uses the left hand to apply traction on the gallbladder, facilitating the separation from the liver while maintaining a clear areolar plane to prevent rupture or bleeding.

The surgeon has separated half of the gallbladder and approaches the fundus. The direction of the 30-degree laparoscopic telescope is adjusted downward, and the fundus grasper is removed as the left hand provides traction, with the light cable positioned downward.

With the 30-degree telescope positioned with the light cable down, the surgeon visualizes the fossa from below upwards, clearly observing the plane between the gallbladder and liver. The remaining fundus of the gallbladder is separated from the liver.
The surgeon examines the Calot's fossa and liver, checking for the presence of the schistohepatic duct, clips, and knots before completing the final part of the dissection.

The surgeon examines the liver surface from where the gallbladder has been separated, checking for any bile leaks, presence of the duct of Luschka, cystohepatic duct, or open sinuses. Fulgration may be performed using a monopolar instrument if necessary.

The surgeon uses coagulation current from a distance and occasionally employs the harmonic device in minimum mode to scrub over the liver surface, effectively stopping any visible oozing from the liver tissue.
The surgeon hooks and detaches the final part of the gallbladder from the liver, completing the cholecystectomy. As the last section is cut, the liver visibly falls back into place.

The surgeon inserts a 10 mm claw forceps through the epigastric port to grasp the neck of the gallbladder, where a clip has been applied over the cystic duct. The forceps hold this area securely to prevent any transverse arrest, preparing to conceal the cystic duct.
The surgeon removes the cannula and extracts the gallbladder from the abdominal cavity, completing the laparoscopic cholecystectomy procedure.
